name: Astrology
shortDescription: Babylonian astrology was a system of divination based on the belief that celestial bodies influenced earthly events and human affairs. It involved interpreting the positions and movements of the sun, moon, planets, and stars to predict the future and understand the present.
description: Babylonian astrology, emerging in the 2nd millennium BCE, represents a sophisticated system of divination deeply intertwined with Mesopotamian cosmology and religion. Unlike modern astrology, which often focuses on individual horoscopes and personality traits, Babylonian astrology was primarily concerned with predicting the fate of the king and the nation. The Babylonians meticulously observed the movements of celestial bodies, believing that these movements directly reflected the will of the gods and their influence on earthly affairs. Key components included the zodiac, which was divided into twelve signs, each associated with specific deities and characteristics. The positions of the sun, moon, and planets within these signs were carefully recorded and interpreted by trained priests and scholars. The Babylonians developed elaborate systems for interpreting omens derived from celestial phenomena, such as eclipses, planetary conjunctions, and the appearance of comets. These omens were believed to portend significant events, such as wars, famines, and the rise or fall of rulers. Prominent deities like Shamash (sun god), Marduk (chief god of Babylon), and Ea (god of wisdom and water) were associated with specific celestial bodies and played crucial roles in the interpretation of astrological omens. The practice of extispicy (examining the entrails of sacrificed animals) was often combined with astrological observations to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the divine will. The development of Babylonian astrology laid the foundation for later astrological traditions in Greece and other parts of the ancient world. Its influence can be seen in the development of Hellenistic astrology, which incorporated elements of Babylonian and Egyptian cosmology.

sources.title: The Ancient Near East: An Anthology of Texts and Pictures
sources.author: James B. Pritchard

sources.title: Astrology and Astronomy in Mesopotamian Culture
sources.author: Hermann Hunger and David Pingree
